questions.question: Which breed is the most common backyard chicken?
questions.funFact: Rhode Island Reds are hardy and friendly and can lay up to 300 brown eggs per year.

questions.question: What breed has fluffy feathers that look like fur?
questions.funFact: Silkies have a unique gene that prevents their feathers from interlocking giving them a silky soft feel.

questions.question: Which breed is famous for being very large and calm?
questions.funFact: Brahma chickens can weigh up to 5.5kg and were once called the King of Chickens.

questions.question: What is a Bantam chicken known for?
questions.funFact: Bantams are typically one-fifth to one-quarter the size of standard chickens.

questions.question: Which breed lays blue or green eggs?
questions.funFact: Araucana chickens originated in Chile and carry a gene that deposits pigment into the eggshell.

questions.question: Which breed holds the world record for most eggs in a year?
questions.funFact: An ISA Brown hen once laid 365 eggs in 364 days — almost one every single day.

questions.question: What breed is known for black feathers and Australian origin?
questions.funFact: The Australorp set the world egg-laying record in 1922 with 364 eggs in 365 days.

questions.question: Which ancient breed is considered one of the oldest domesticated chickens?
questions.funFact: The Red Jungle Fowl is the wild ancestor of all domestic chickens first domesticated around 7000 BC.

questions.question: What is a Frizzle chicken known for?
questions.funFact: Frizzle feathers curl outward and upward due to a single dominant gene mutation.

questions.question: Which dual-purpose breed was developed in the USA in the 1800s and named after a state?
questions.funFact: Rhode Island Red was developed in Massachusetts and Rhode Island and became the official state bird of Rhode Island.

questions.question: How many days does a chicken egg take to hatch?
questions.funFact: The embryo turns itself over 50 times per day inside the egg during incubation.

questions.question: What colours do most common chickens lay?
questions.funFact: Shell colour is determined entirely by breed genetics not diet or environment.

questions.question: What do you call keeping a fertilised egg warm to hatch?
questions.funFact: The ideal incubation temperature is 37.5°C — even 1°C off can affect hatch rates.

questions.question: Which part of the egg protects the developing chick?
questions.funFact: An eggshell has up to 17000 tiny pores that allow oxygen in and carbon dioxide out.

questions.question: What determines the colour of a chicken's eggshell?
questions.funFact: You can predict a hen's egg colour by looking at her earlobes — red earlobes usually mean brown eggs.

questions.question: At what age do most hens begin laying eggs?
questions.funFact: This first lay is called point of lay and varies by breed — some take up to 32 weeks.

questions.question: What is the small white spot on a yolk called?
questions.funFact: The blastodisc is where fertilisation occurs — if fertilised it becomes the blastoderm.

questions.question: How many eggs can a productive hen lay per year?
questions.funFact: Production drops by around 10-15% each year after the first laying season.

questions.question: What string-like structure keeps the yolk centred in the egg?
questions.funFact: The chalaza is a twisted strand of egg white that anchors the yolk — thicker means a fresher egg.

questions.question: Which nutrient in egg yolk is essential for brain development?
questions.funFact: One egg yolk contains about 147mg of choline — roughly 27% of the recommended daily intake.

questions.question: What is the main ingredient in standard chicken feed?
questions.funFact: Corn provides energy while soybean meal provides the protein chickens need to lay eggs.

questions.question: How often should chickens have access to fresh water?
questions.funFact: A laying hen drinks about 500ml of water per day — more in hot weather.

questions.question: What common food is DANGEROUS for chickens?
questions.funFact: Avocado contains persin a fungicidal toxin that causes heart failure in birds.

questions.question: What nutrient do chickens need most for strong eggshells?
questions.funFact: Crushed oyster shells are the most popular calcium supplement for laying hens.

questions.question: What is grit used for in chicken digestion?
questions.funFact: Chickens have no teeth — they swallow grit which helps their muscular gizzard grind up food.

questions.question: How much feed does an average laying hen eat per day?
questions.funFact: Free-range hens eat less commercial feed because they supplement with insects and grass.

questions.question: Which kitchen scrap should never be fed to chickens?
questions.funFact: Raw potato skins contain solanine a toxic glycoalkaloid that can harm chickens.

questions.question: What feed type is best for chicks in their first weeks?
questions.funFact: Starter crumble has 20-24% protein — layer pellets have only 16% and too much calcium for chicks.

questions.question: What mineral deficiency causes soft or thin eggshells?
questions.funFact: Hens require 4g of calcium per day for eggshell production — deficiency causes shell-less eggs.

questions.question: Why do some keepers add apple cider vinegar to chicken water?
questions.funFact: Apple cider vinegar lowers water pH making it inhospitable to harmful bacteria and promoting good gut flora.

questions.question: What is the most visible sign a chicken is unwell?
questions.funFact: Healthy chickens are alert and active — a sick hen will separate herself from the flock.

questions.question: What are mites and lice on chickens classified as?
questions.funFact: Red mites feed on chickens at night and hide in coop cracks during the day.

questions.question: What should you do if a chicken sneezes frequently?
questions.funFact: Frequent sneezing can indicate respiratory infection — early isolation prevents flock spread.

questions.question: What is Marek's Disease?
questions.funFact: Marek's Disease is caused by a herpesvirus and can be prevented with vaccination at hatch.

questions.question: What condition causes a hen's abdomen to swell with fluid?
questions.funFact: Ascites is caused by heart or liver failure leading to fluid accumulation in the abdomen.

questions.question: What is bumblefoot in chickens?
questions.funFact: Bumblefoot is caused by Staphylococcus bacteria entering through cuts — rough perches are a common cause.

questions.question: What does egg binding mean?
options: A hen lays too many eggs
A hen cannot pass an egg
questions.funFact: A hen with egg binding can die within 48 hours — warm baths and calcium supplements can help.

questions.question: What parasite causes coccidiosis in young chicks?
questions.funFact: Coccidiosis kills more young chicks than almost any other disease — wet litter accelerates its spread.

questions.question: Which disease causes tumours on nerves and organs of chickens?
questions.funFact: Marek's Disease can also cause paralysis of legs and wings and eye tumours leading to blindness.

questions.question: What is the correct term for inflammation of a hen's oviduct?
questions.funFact: Salpingitis causes the oviduct to fill with a caseous mass called a lash egg — a serious condition.

questions.question: How do chickens communicate with each other?
questions.funFact: Hens use a specific cluck to call chicks to food and a different alarm call for aerial vs ground predators.

questions.question: What is a male chicken called?
questions.funFact: A male under 1 year is a cockerel — over 1 year he becomes a rooster.

questions.question: Approximately how many chickens are there in the world?
questions.funFact: There are more chickens on Earth than any other bird — roughly 4.5 chickens per human.

questions.question: What is a group of chickens called?
questions.funFact: A group of chicks specifically is called a brood and a group with a rooster is called a peep.

questions.question: Can chickens recognise human faces?
questions.funFact: Chickens can distinguish between familiar and unfamiliar humans and remember faces long-term.

questions.question: What is the red fleshy growth on top of a chicken's head?
questions.funFact: The comb helps regulate body temperature — blood circulates through it to release heat.

questions.question: How fast can a chicken run at full speed?
questions.funFact: Chickens can also fly short distances — up to 3 metres high and 50 metres distance.

questions.question: What is the average lifespan of a backyard chicken?
questions.funFact: The oldest recorded chicken Muffy lived to 23 years old in the USA.

questions.question: What is the scientific name for the domestic chicken?
questions.funFact: Gallus gallus domesticus descended from the Red Jungle Fowl Gallus gallus native to Southeast Asia.

questions.question: Which country has the highest chicken-to-human ratio?
questions.funFact: Guyana has approximately 4.4 chickens per person driven by its large poultry farming industry.

questions.question: Which breed is famous for its heavily feathered feet and gentle temperament?
questions.funFact: Cochin chickens were brought from China to England in the 1840s and caused a chicken-keeping craze known as Hen Fever.

questions.question: What colour eggs do White Leghorn chickens typically lay?
questions.funFact: Leghorns are highly efficient layers and are the primary breed used in commercial egg production worldwide.

questions.question: Which British breed is well known for being excessively broody and a devoted mother hen?
questions.funFact: Buff Orpingtons are so reliably broody that keepers often use them as surrogate mothers for other breeds' eggs.

questions.question: Which breed is identified by its distinctive barred black-and-white feather pattern?
questions.funFact: The barred pattern in Plymouth Rocks is caused by a sex-linked gene — making it one of the most studied feather colour patterns in genetics.

questions.question: What type of comb does the Wyandotte breed have, making it cold-hardy?
questions.funFact: The rose comb sits flat to the head reducing frostbite risk — making Wyandottes an ideal choice for cold-climate keepers.

questions.question: Which breed is prized for laying very dark chocolate-brown eggs?
questions.funFact: Marans eggs are so prized in French cuisine that chef Julia Child kept Marans chickens specifically for their dark beautiful eggs.

questions.question: Which exotic Indonesian breed has completely black feathers, skin, meat, and bones?
questions.funFact: Ayam Cemani's all-black colouring is caused by a genetic condition called fibromelanosis which triggers hyperpigmentation throughout the body.

questions.question: What unique anatomical feature does the ancient Dorking breed have?
questions.funFact: The Dorking's fifth toe is non-functional but has been a defining trait since Roman times — Julius Caesar reportedly brought them to Britain.

questions.question: Which American breed was developed by crossing New Hampshire and Barred Plymouth Rock chickens?
questions.funFact: The Delaware breed was nearly extinct by the 1950s when commercial broiler production shifted to Cornish Cross hybrids but was revived by heritage breed enthusiasts.

questions.question: Which French breed holds a protected designation of origin and is considered the world's finest table bird?
questions.funFact: Poulet de Bresse has AOC status like Champagne wine. Authentic birds have blue feet, white feathers, and a red comb — representing the French tricolour flag.

questions.question: What is the natural protective coating on a freshly laid egg called?
questions.funFact: The bloom seals the egg's 17,000 pores to prevent bacteria entering. Washing removes it — which is why US refrigerated eggs differ from unwashed European eggs.

questions.question: What causes a double yolk egg?
options: Two hens laying in the same nest
A genetic defect in the shell
Very old or very young hens only
questions.funFact: Double yolk eggs occur about once in every 1,000 eggs and are most common in young hens whose laying cycle hasn't yet fully regulated.

questions.question: How does the air cell inside an egg change as the egg gets older?
questions.funFact: This is why a fresh egg sinks flat in water and a stale egg floats — moisture evaporates through the pores and the air cell expands over time.

questions.question: What gives egg yolks their yellow-orange colour?
questions.funFact: Hens fed marigold petals or carotenoid-rich plants produce deep orange yolks with higher levels of lutein and zeaxanthin — beneficial antioxidants.

questions.question: Which simple test confirms whether a raw egg is fresh without cracking it open?
questions.funFact: A fresh egg sinks and lies flat. A week-old egg tilts upward. A bad egg floats — its air cell has expanded too much.

questions.question: How many calories does an average large egg contain?
questions.funFact: Despite its small size an egg contains 13 essential vitamins and minerals — making it one of the most nutrient-dense foods per calorie available.

questions.question: What approximate percentage of an egg's total weight is the shell?
questions.funFact: The shell's calcium carbonate lattice is strong enough to bear the hen's weight yet fragile enough for a chick to break from inside with just its beak.

questions.question: Which protein found in raw egg whites binds the B-vitamin biotin, preventing its absorption?
questions.funFact: Cooking egg whites deactivates avidin. Eating large amounts of raw egg whites regularly can cause biotin deficiency — historically called egg white injury.

questions.question: What is the innermost protective membrane lining the inside of an eggshell called?
questions.funFact: There are two shell membranes — an outer and an inner. The air cell forms between them at the broad end of the egg immediately after laying as the egg cools.

questions.question: Which rare egg abnormality occurs when a fully formed egg ends up inside another egg?
questions.funFact: This extremely rare phenomenon happens when a second ovulation occurs shortly after the first egg forms. The first egg reverses back up the oviduct and becomes encased in the second egg.

questions.question: What protein-rich treat do chickens love that also helps boost feather regrowth during moult?
questions.funFact: Mealworms are 53% protein by dry weight — perfect during moult when hens need extra protein to regrow their feathers quickly.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a dust bath for chickens?
questions.funFact: Chickens instinctively dust bathe — fine sand or dry soil particles penetrate feathers and smother mites and lice by blocking their breathing pores.

questions.question: What brooder temperature is recommended for chicks in their very first week of life?
questions.funFact: Chicks cannot regulate their own body temperature for the first few weeks. The brooder temperature is reduced by about 3°C each week until they are fully feathered.

questions.question: At what age are chicks typically ready to move from the brooder to an outdoor coop?
questions.funFact: Chicks are ready when fully feathered which takes 6-8 weeks. Transitioning during warm weather reduces cold stress and makes integration easier.

questions.question: What is fermented chicken feed and why is it beneficial?
options: Feed soaked in water to grow beneficial bacteria
questions.funFact: Fermented feed improves nutrient bioavailability by up to 30% and contains probiotics that support gut health — hens often eat less but absorb more.

questions.question: Which herb placed in nesting boxes acts as a natural relaxant and pest deterrent?
questions.funFact: Lavender contains linalool which repels insects and has a calming effect on hens. Mint and rosemary are also effective natural pest deterrents in the coop.

questions.question: What compound in onions can damage a chicken's red blood cells in large quantities?
questions.funFact: N-propyl disulfide oxidises haemoglobin in red blood cells causing Heinz body anaemia. Small occasional amounts are usually harmless but large doses can be fatal.

questions.question: What is the minimum recommended outdoor run space per standard-sized chicken?
questions.funFact: Overcrowding causes stress, feather pecking, and disease spread. More space per bird leads to healthier happier chickens and consistently better egg production.

questions.question: Why should raw dried beans never be fed to chickens?
options: They contain phytohaemagglutinin which is highly toxic
They are too hard to digest
questions.funFact: Phytohaemagglutinin (PHA) in raw beans — especially kidney beans — can be lethal to chickens within hours. Thorough cooking destroys the toxin completely.

questions.question: What feeding practice called scratch encourages chickens' natural foraging behaviour?
options: Hand feeding treats one by one
Scattering mixed grains on the ground
questions.funFact: Scratch grains are lower in protein than complete feed and should only make up about 10% of a hen's diet — too much scratch leads to nutritional imbalances.

questions.question: What is the most important first step when adding new chickens to an existing flock?
options: Introduce them at night so they wake up together
Put them in the same coop immediately
questions.funFact: Quarantine allows you to observe new birds for illness. Many diseases have incubation periods of 1-2 weeks — a full 4-week quarantine is the safest standard.

questions.question: What colour change in a chicken's comb can signal circulatory problems or cold stress?
questions.funFact: A blue or purple comb indicates cyanosis — poor blood oxygenation. It can signal heart failure, severe respiratory disease, or extreme cold exposure.

questions.question: What is the best way to tell whether a chicken has lice rather than mites?
options: Lice are visible on the bird during the day while mites hide in coop cracks
Mites are active during the day and lice at night
questions.funFact: Red mites are blood-suckers that leave the bird during the day and hide in cracks. Checking the coop at night with a torch reveals them. Lice live permanently on the bird.

questions.question: What respiratory disease in chickens is caused by Mycoplasma gallisepticum bacteria?
questions.funFact: CRD spreads through direct contact and contaminated equipment causing rattling breathing, swollen sinuses, and reduced egg production. Stress is a common trigger.

questions.question: What is a prolapsed vent in chickens?
options: Internal tissue protruding from the cloaca
questions.funFact: Vent prolapse is most common in young hens that begin laying too early or lay very large eggs. Keeping the area moist and temporarily isolating the bird can help mild cases.

questions.question: What is the most common internal parasite found in backyard chickens?
questions.funFact: Ascaridia galli roundworms can grow up to 11cm long. Severe infections cause weight loss, pale combs, and reduced laying. Regular faecal checks and deworming are recommended.

questions.question: Which vitamin deficiency causes encephalomalacia known as crazy chick disease?
questions.funFact: Vitamin E acts as an antioxidant that protects brain tissue. Deficiency causes neurological symptoms including head tremors and loss of coordination in young chicks.

questions.question: What fungal infection affects a hen's cloaca causing white discharge and a foul smell?
questions.funFact: Vent gleet is caused by Candida yeast overgrowth triggered by stress, antibiotic overuse, or poor diet. It is treated with antifungal medication and probiotic supplementation.

questions.question: Which viral disease causes twisted neck syndrome known as wry neck or torticollis in chickens?
questions.funFact: Newcastle Disease affects the nervous system causing head tremors, wing drooping, and twisted neck. It has a very high mortality rate in unvaccinated flocks worldwide.

questions.question: What rare condition can cause a hen to spontaneously develop male physical characteristics like spurs and crowing?
questions.funFact: If a hen's left ovary is damaged the right rudimentary gonad can develop into a testis. The hen may grow spurs, develop a rooster-style comb, and even begin crowing.

questions.question: What distinctive sound does a hen typically make immediately after laying an egg?
options: The egg song — loud repeated clucking
questions.funFact: The egg song can last up to 15 minutes and the whole flock often joins in. Scientists believe it may signal the hen's health and productivity to the rest of the flock.

questions.question: What is the name of the red fleshy skin hanging below a chicken's beak?
questions.funFact: Wattles help regulate body temperature just like the comb — blood circulates through them releasing heat. Roosters generally have larger wattles than hens.

questions.question: What is a young female chicken under one year old called?
questions.funFact: A pullet begins laying between 18-24 weeks depending on breed. Her first eggs are often smaller and irregular until her laying cycle fully regulates.

questions.question: Do chickens experience REM sleep?
options: No, they only have light sleep
Yes, they cycle through REM and slow-wave sleep
questions.funFact: Studies show chickens have full REM sleep cycles with rapid eye movements — the same stage in which humans dream. They often sleep with one eye open to watch for predators.

questions.question: How do mother hens begin communicating with chicks while they are still inside the egg?
options: They cluck softly and the chick responds with peeps
questions.funFact: Hen-chick communication begins 3 days before hatching. The chick's peeps from inside the egg help the mother hen coordinate hatching across all eggs in the clutch.

questions.question: What special visual ability do chickens have that humans lack?
questions.funFact: Chickens have four types of colour receptors compared to humans' three — allowing them to see UV light and detect feather markings and egg patterns invisible to the human eye.

questions.question: Why do roosters crow at dawn even before sunrise?
options: They respond to other roosters crowing first
It is a territorial display triggered by light
Their internal circadian clock anticipates the light cycle
questions.funFact: Roosters have an internal circadian clock that triggers crowing before sunrise. Even in total darkness with no time cues they will still crow at the expected dawn time.

questions.question: Approximately how many taste buds does a chicken have compared to a human's roughly 10,000?
questions.funFact: Chickens have very few taste buds but are sensitive to salt and bitter compounds. Capsaicin from chilli peppers doesn't affect them at all — making chilli a useful deterrent for other animals.

questions.question: According to evolutionary science, which came first — the chicken or the egg?
questions.funFact: Animals laid eggs for hundreds of millions of years before chickens evolved. The first chicken egg was laid by a proto-chicken bird whose genetic mutation produced the first true chicken.

questions.question: Approximately how many years ago were chickens first domesticated according to archaeological evidence?
questions.funFact: Archaeological evidence from China and Southeast Asia dates chicken domestication to around 6000 BCE. They were initially kept for cockfighting and religious ceremonies — not food.
